attributes.stem:
An otherwise well 32-year-old woman presents with a mole on her left arm. This lesion has been present for two months and is increasing in size. She has several other moles, but they have not changed for many years. She spends about 30 minutes a day on outside activities and does use sunscreen. She gets a light tan in the sun. There is no history of skin cancer nor family history of melanoma.

On full skin examination she has a Fitzpatrick skin phototype 2. The lesion on her arm was examined with a dermatoscope and shows an asymmetrical skin lesion with three colours and an atypical pigment network. Excisional biopsy is performed, with the histopathology report as follows:

'Superficial spreading melanoma. Breslow depth 1.5mm, Clark level 3. No ulceration, no satellite lesions, no dermal mitoses seen. Margins clear, closest lateral 2mm at 12 o'clock position.'

Wide local excision was performed with sentinel node biopsy of the axillary nodes and shows no evidence of residual/metastatic tumour.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Immediately repeat full skin examination to exclude any other lesions of concern
Full skin examination for surveillance in 3-6 months
attributes.explanation:
Melanoma is extremely common with Australia having the highest melanoma rates in the world due to a multitude of reasons, making the identification of melanoma a vital skill to have. 

The idea of Breslow depth is an important concept to grasp as it decides the excision width and also the risk of spreading to nearby nodes and also prognosis. In this case the tumour is 1.5mm and would require 1-2 cm of excision due to the possibility of metastatic spread to regional lymph nodes being 15-25%. This warrants a sentinel lymph node biopsy. The lymph node biopsy is an important predictor of melanoma specific survival for any melanoma having a thickness >1mm. Any tumour <1mm has a likelihood of lymph node involvement at the most 10%, where lymph node biopsy is only warranted in some cases for fear of microscopic involvement. Overall, someone with a negative sentinel lymph node biopsy will have a 5-year survival rate of 90% compared to a positive one of 72%. Other than that, there are risk calculators that can further predict the risk in 10 years, which if calculated would bring a 10-year risk of developing another melanoma at ~9%.

The patient has stage IIa based on the AJCC 8th edition. Current AJCC guidelines do not recommend any staging test, CT/PET scan at stage 1 or stage 2 as it will often have low detection rate, does not bring survival benefits and can even result in false-positives that might even be harmful. Current data suggest that PET/CT only is needed in the cases of staging anyone with extensive nodal disease and is other main indication is to check for response to treatment or search for other lesions that might potentially be melanoma that allows for further investigation.

Follow up can be guided by the AJCC guidelines but overall, there is no standardised follow-up period based on current evidence and the treating physician will have to decide using the patient's individual risk.

  • Stage I: annually

  • Stage IIA: every 6 months for 2 years, then annually

  • Stage IIB and IIC: every 3 to 4 months for 2 years, every 6 months in year 3, then annually

  • Stage IIIA to IIIC: every 3 months for 2 years, every 6 months during year 3, then annually.

What clinical surveillance does is search for symptoms suggestive of metastases and education of patients to ensure that the risk recurrence or metastases is minimal. A total body-skin examination can also be offered in those who are stage IIA and above or depending on risk factors.

Generally, the peak recurrence period is in the first three years with a median time of 16 months although late recurrence up to 20 years can happen. It should be noted as this woman does have melanoma; every immediate family member should have regular skin surveillance.

Immediate full skin examination is not wrong but not the best option from the choices available.
learningPoints: Clinical surveillance and follow up after a diagnosis of melanoma should be adjusted according to the individual patient risk of recurrence/developing subsequent primary melanoma, taking into account that most recurrence/subsequent primaries are diagnosed within the first year of initial diagnosis
A negative sentinel node biopsy has a great negative predictive value and imaging is not required
Stage 1 and stage 2 melanoma generally doesnt need imaging
Melanoma Institute of Australia risk calculator for melanoma:  https://www.melanomarisk.org.au
references: Thompson JF, Scolyer RA, Kefford RF. Melanoma [Internet]. Australian Family Physician. The Royal Australian College of general Practitioners; 2012 [cited 2022Nov1]. Available from: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2012/july/melanoma-guide
Melanoma [Internet]. Therapeutic Guidelines [digital]. 2022 [cited 2022Nov1]. Available from: https://tgldcdp.tg.org.au/etgAccess

Buzaid AC, Gershenwald JE. Staging work-up and surveillance of cutaneous melanoma [Internet]. UpToDate. 2022 [cited 2022Nov1]. Available from: https://www.uptodate.com/contents/staging-work-up-and-surveillance-of-cutaneous-melanoma

Mar VJ, Soyer HP, Button-Sloan A, Fishburn   P. Diagnosis and management of cutaneous melanoma [Internet]. Australian Journal of General Practice. 2020 [cited 2022Nov1]. Available from: https://www1.racgp.org.au/ajgp/2020/november/diagnosis-and-management-of-cutaneous-melanoma
